items.reality: DL models optimize statistical objectives on training data. They learn useful representations and patterns but do not inherently possess understanding, intent, or grounded world models unless explicitly designed and validated for those properties.
items.example: A language model can fluently discuss medicine yet confidently hallucinate dosages — pattern matching without verified reasoning.

items.myth: You always need more layers to get better results.
items.reality: Depth helps hierarchical structure but must match data, task, and regularization. Too many layers without enough data or proper training tricks causes optimization difficulty and overfitting.
items.example: A three-layer MLP can beat an over-deep unregularized stack on a small tabular benchmark.

items.myth: Deep learning replaced classical machine learning.
items.reality: Classical methods remain strong on structured tabular data, small datasets, and interpretability requirements. DL dominates many unstructured modalities but complements rather than replaces the ML toolbox.
items.example: Kaggle tabular competitions still frequently top leaderboards with gradient boosting, not giant neural nets.
